narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection from our law school admissions process. Our model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 77% chance that you would fail it on your first try, which is the key threshold we use to make admission decisions—applications with high predicted failure risks like yours are unfortunately denied to ensure we select students likely to succeed in their legal careers. The primary reason for this prediction was your Year 3 school grade decile of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and had the strongest influence pushing the model toward forecasting bar exam failure. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed notably in the same direction, signaling early academic challenges compared to the typical student's mid-range performance in those years. While your middle family income bracket played a supporting role in elevating the risk slightly above average, it wasn't the dominant factor. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, very close to the dataset average of 36.7, strongly pulled the prediction toward success, showing your potential in standardized testing. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, a bit below the typical 3.21, nudged things toward higher risk but with less impact, and your choice of full-time study aligned with the vast majority of students (over 92%) and helped counterbalance the concerns mildly. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students mainly due to those lower grade deciles, which carry significant weight in how the model assesses long-term bar passage odds for someone with your background. This isn't a judgment on your character or drive—many strong candidates face similar hurdles and pivot successfully. To move forward, we recommend retaking courses or seeking academic coaching to boost your decile rankings, perhaps exploring part-time options if that fits better, and reapplying once you've strengthened those areas, as even small improvements can shift predictions dramatically. You're clearly capable, especially with that LSAT, and with targeted effort, law school doors can reopen for you. Keep pushing—we're rooting for your success.
